subject: Science
read_aloud: How many planets are in our solar system? Can you name the eight planets?
correct_answers: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune
correct_response: Brilliant! There are exactly eight planets in our solar system! You're a space explorer!
incorrect_response: Not quite. There are eight planets in our solar system. Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. Can you name them?
hint: Think of the planets orbiting our Sun. Count them out!
metacognitive_prompt: How did you remember there are eight planets? Did you count them or use a memory trick?

subject: Science
read_aloud: Which planet is closest to the Sun? Is it A, Mercury, Venus, Earth, or Mars?
correct_response: Yes! Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun! Fantastic space knowledge!
incorrect_response: Not right. The closest planet to the Sun is Mercury. Mercury is first, then Venus, Earth, then Mars. They get hotter as you get closer to the Sun!
hint: The planets go in order from the Sun: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune. Which is first?
metacognitive_prompt: Did you remember the order of planets from the Sun? What helped you decide?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What's the biggest planet in our solar system? Which one has the most giant storms?
correct_response: Spot on! Jupiter is the biggest planet in our solar system! Its famous Great Red Spot is bigger than Earth!
incorrect_response: Nearly! Jupiter is the biggest planet. It's a gas giant, much larger than Earth, Mars, or Venus. The Great Red Spot storm is massive!
hint: Think about the planets you learned. Which one did I say is huge and has giant storms?
metacognitive_prompt: What clue helped you remember Jupiter is the biggest planet?

subject: Science
read_aloud: True or false: Earth is the only planet that has liquid water on its surface.
correct_response: That's true! Earth is special because we have oceans, lakes, and rivers - liquid water! Our blue planet!
incorrect_response: Actually, this is true! Earth is the only planet in our solar system with liquid water on its surface. The other planets are either too hot, too cold, or made of gas, so they can't have liquid water. Earth is unique!
hint: Think about the other planets. Mercury and Venus are super hot. Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune are gas giants. Only Earth has oceans and lakes!
mnemonic: Earth is the blue planet with water!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember the other planets can't have liquid water?

subject: Science
read_aloud: How long does it take for Earth to orbit the Sun once? Is it A, 365 days, B, 1 year, or C, 7 years?
correct_response: Excellent! It takes Earth 365 days to complete one orbit around the Sun - that's one whole year! You're becoming a space scientist!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Earth takes 365 days to orbit the Sun once. That's exactly one year. It's not 7 years, which is way too long, and it's not 1 day, which is way too short!
hint: Earth takes one full year to go around the Sun once. 365 days!
metacognitive_prompt: How did you work out that Earth takes a full year to orbit?

subject: Science
read_aloud: Which planet has the most extreme temperatures? Is it A, Mercury, Venus, or Mars?
correct_response: Yes! Venus is the hottest planet in our solar system! Its thick clouds trap heat and make it super hot - up to 465°C!
incorrect_response: Nearly! Venus is the hottest planet. Mercury gets very hot during the day but cools down at night. Venus has thick clouds that trap heat, keeping it boiling hot all the time!
hint: Think about the planets with atmospheres. Venus has thick clouds like a blanket. Mercury is bare rock and has no blanket to keep the heat in!
mnemonic: Venus has thick clouds that trap heat!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember Venus is hotter than Mercury?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What force pulls us down towards the ground? It's what makes things fall when you drop them!
correct_response: Brilliant! Gravity is the force that pulls us down to Earth! It's gravity that makes your pencil drop when you let go of it!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Gravity is the force that pulls things down toward the ground. When you jump up, gravity pulls you back down. When you drop a ball, gravity pulls it down. It's an invisible force everywhere on Earth!
hint: Think about what happens when you jump. You go up, then come back down. What invisible force pulls you back down?
mnemonic: Gravity pulls everything down!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other things gravity pulls down? Like rain falling from the sky?

subject: Science
read_aloud: True or false: On the Moon, you would weigh much less than on Earth because the Moon has weaker gravity than Earth.
correct_response: That's true! The Moon's gravity is only about one-sixth as strong as Earth's, so you'd weigh much less up there! Astronauts can jump really high on the Moon!
incorrect_response: Actually, this is true! The Moon is much smaller than Earth, so its gravity is weaker - only about 1/6th of Earth's gravity. That means if you weigh 60kg on Earth, you'd only weigh about 10kg on the Moon. You could jump three times higher!
hint: The Moon is smaller than Earth - about 1/4 the size. Smaller size means weaker gravity. Think about how much lighter you'd be on the Moon!
mnemonic: Moon gravity is weaker - you'd be lighter!
metacognitive_prompt: Did you know the Moon is smaller than Earth? What helped you remember?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What force makes it harder to push a heavy box than a light box? Is it A, friction, magnetism, or gravity?
correct_response: Spot on! Friction is the force that makes things rub against each other and resist movement! That's why it's hard to slide a heavy box across the carpet - friction fights back!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is friction. Friction is the rubbing force between two surfaces. When you push a heavy box, the rough bottom rubs against the carpet and creates friction, making it hard to move. Gravity pulls down, but friction resists the sliding!
hint: Think about trying to push a heavy box across a rough surface like a carpet. What force fights against your pushing?
mnemonic: Friction is the rubbing force!
metacognitive_prompt: Have you ever tried to push something heavy across a rough carpet? What did you feel?

subject: Science
read_aloud: A ball rolling down a hill speeds up. Which force makes this happen? Is it A, gravity pulling, friction slowing, or magnetism?
correct_response: Yes! Gravity pulls the ball down the hill, making it speed up! Gravity always pulls things towards the centre of Earth!
incorrect_response: Not right. The force that makes the ball speed up is gravity. Gravity pulls things down towards Earth's centre. As the ball goes down the hill, gravity accelerates it, making it faster and faster!
hint: Think about what happens when you drop or roll something downhill. What invisible force pulls everything down?
mnemonic: Gravity pulls down - things speed up on slopes!
metacognitive_prompt: What clue helped you identify gravity? Did you think about rolling downhill?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call a force that can push or pull without touching? You can use it to pick up paperclips or make a toy car move without touching it!
correct_response: Brilliant! Magnetism is the invisible force that can push or pull without touching! Magnets attract iron and steel - that's why they stick to your fridge!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is magnetism. Magnetism is a force that acts at a distance. Magnets have north and south poles. When you bring a magnet near something magnetic like paperclips or a toy car, they attract each other and pull together without touching!
hint: Think about magnets. They have two ends - a north end and a south end. They attract certain metals. What can they do without touching?
mnemonic: Magnets attract without touching!
metacognitive_prompt: Have you ever played with magnets? What happens when they get close to things?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What simple machine helps you go up and down between floors? Hint: you stand on moving steps!
correct_response: Excellent! A lift or elevator carries people up and down between floors! It uses a motor and cables to pull against gravity!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is a lift or elevator. This machine has a platform that moves up and down between floors, carrying people. It fights against gravity to lift you up, and uses brakes and safety systems to bring you down safely!
hint: Think about tall buildings. What machine carries people up and down? It has buttons and a motor!
mnemonic: Lifts and elevators go up and down!
metacognitive_prompt: Have you ever been in a tall building? What helps you go between floors?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What simple machine has a sharp blade that spins around fast to cut grass, paper, or your hair? You might use one on your lawn!
correct_response: Perfect! A lawn mower has a spinning blade that cuts grass! The blade spins really fast to slice through the grass!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is a lawn mower. This machine has a blade that spins around to cut things. It's used on lawns to keep grass short. The blade is sharp and fast - zoom!
hint: Think about cutting grass. What machine has a spinning blade that you might use outside?
mnemonic: Lawn mowers spin to cut grass!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember the lawn mower? Did you think about cutting or spinning blades?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What energy do we get from food? Food gives us energy to run, jump, and play! Is it kinetic, potential, or chemical?
correct_response: Brilliant! Kinetic energy is the energy of motion - the energy things have when they're moving! When you run, jump, or play, your body is moving and has kinetic energy!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is kinetic. Kinetic energy is movement energy. When something moves, it has kinetic energy. When you run around, you have kinetic energy. When a ball rolls, it has kinetic energy. Anything that moves has this energy!
hint: Think about moving things. When you run, your body is in motion. What do we call that energy?
mnemonic: Moving things have kinetic energy!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you give me an example of something moving that has kinetic energy?

subject: Science
read_aloud: True or false: Energy can never be created or destroyed - it only changes from one form to another.
correct_response: That's true! Energy can never be created or destroyed - it's conserved! The same energy that existed before always exists, just in a different form! This is one of the most important rules in science!
incorrect_response: Actually, this is true! Energy can never be created or destroyed. It can change forms - like electricity to heat, or chemical to light - but the total amount of energy stays the same. You can't create energy from nothing, and you can't make energy disappear!
hint: Think about energy changing forms. Electricity changes to heat and light. Batteries turn chemical energy into electricity. Does the total energy ever disappear?
mnemonic: Energy is never created or destroyed!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of energy changing from one form to another? Like batteries or lightbulbs?

subject: Science
read_aloud: A light bulb transforms electrical energy into light energy. What do we call the original energy in the wire (before it becomes light)? Is it A, electrical, chemical, or potential?
correct_response: Yes! Electrical energy flows through the wire to the light bulb! The bulb transforms it into bright light! This is how we light up our homes - electricity flows from power stations through wires to light bulbs!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is electrical. Before electricity flows into the bulb, it's electrical energy in the wire. When the bulb lights up, that's light energy. The original energy form was electrical - it's the energy of moving electrons in a wire!
hint: Think about what powers a light bulb. Electricity flows from the wall through a wire. What type of energy is in the wire before it reaches the bulb?
mnemonic: Wires carry electrical energy to light bulbs!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you identify electrical energy? Did you think about what flows through wires?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call animals that only eat plants? Is it A, carnivores, herbivores, or omnivores?
correct_response: Brilliant! Herbivores are animals that only eat plants! Rabbits, cows, deer, and horses are herbivores - they munch on grass and leaves!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is herbivores. Herbivores are plant-eaters. Animals that only eat plants include rabbits, sheep, cows, horses, giraffes, and many insects. Carnivores eat meat, and omnivores eat both!
hint: Think about farm animals you know. Cows, sheep, and horses eat grass and hay. They only eat plants. What do we call plant-eating animals?
mnemonic: Herbivores only eat plants!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you name some herbivores? What helps you remember what they eat?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call animals that only eat other animals? Is it A, carnivores, or predators?
correct_response: Yes! Carnivores are animals that only eat other animals! Lions, tigers, wolves, and sharks hunt and eat meat - they're predators!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is carnivores. Carnivores are meat-eaters. Predators hunt and eat other animals. Lions eat zebras, wolves eat rabbits, sharks eat fish. They don't eat plants!
hint: Think about fierce hunters. Lions, wolves, tigers - they hunt and eat other animals. What do we call meat-eating animals?
mnemonic: Carnivores eat other animals!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember carnivores? Did you think about predators hunting?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call organisms that break down dead plants and animals and turn them back into soil? They're like nature's recyclers!
correct_response: Fantastic! Decomposers break down dead things and return nutrients to the soil! Fungi, bacteria, and worms are decomposers - they turn waste back into soil that helps new plants grow!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is decomposers. Decomposers are nature's recyclers. They break down dead leaves, dead animals, and fallen trees. Tiny organisms like fungi, bacteria, and worms do this important work. They turn waste into soil that new plants can use to grow!
hint: Think about what happens to fallen leaves in a forest. They slowly rot away and disappear. What tiny organisms do this work?
mnemonic: Decomposers break down dead things!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of an example of decomposition? Like leaves rotting in autumn?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call water when it freezes and becomes solid ice? Is it A, solid, liquid, or gas?
correct_response: Brilliant! Water becomes solid ice when it freezes! Ice cubes in your drink, frozen lakes in winter, and ice skating on ponds - it's all solid water!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is solid. When water gets very cold, it freezes and becomes solid ice. Water flows as a liquid but ice is hard and solid. Ice cubes, glaciers, and frozen ponds are solid water!
hint: Think about ice cubes in your drink. What happens to water when it gets really cold?
mnemonic: Frozen water is solid ice!
metacognitive_prompt: Have you ever seen water freeze and turn into ice? What happens to it?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call water when it boils and becomes steam? Is it A, solid, liquid, or gas?
correct_response: Yes! Water becomes gas when it boils! Steam from a kettle, clouds in the sky, and water vapour you can't see - it's all gas!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is gas. When water gets very hot and boils, it turns into gas. This is water vapour or steam. You can see steam rising from a boiling kettle, and clouds are made of tiny water droplets - they're gas!
hint: Think about a boiling kettle. What do you see rising from it? You can't see water vapour, but you know it's there!
mnemonic: Boiling water makes steam gas!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you identify gas? Did you think about steam or clouds?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What process happens when ice melts into liquid water? Is it A, condensation, melting, or evaporation?
correct_response: Excellent! Melting is when ice changes from solid to liquid! Ice cubes melt in your drink, snowmen melt in spring, and glaciers shrink - it's all the melting process!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is melting. Melting is when a solid turns into a liquid. Ice becomes water when it warms up. Snowmen melt, ice cubes disappear in drinks, and frozen lakes turn back into water!
hint: Think about what happens to snow in warm weather. It slowly changes from solid to liquid. What do we call this process?
mnemonic: Solid ice melts into liquid water!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember melting? Did you think about snow or ice cubes disappearing?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call it when a gas changes directly to a liquid without becoming a solid or another gas first? It's skipping a step!
correct_response: Spot on! Condensation is when gas changes directly to liquid! Water vapour in the air cools down and turns into liquid water droplets - like dew on grass or water on a cold window!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is condensation. When water vapour cools down, it can turn back into liquid without first becoming solid or gas again. Dew forms on grass overnight, and water appears on cold windows - that's condensation!
hint: Think about morning dew on grass. It appeared overnight from water vapour in the air. What process is that?
mnemonic: Gas cooling into liquid droplets!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember condensation? Did you think about dew or cold windows?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What's the name of the organ that pumps blood all around your body? It sends blood to your brain, muscles, and everywhere you need it!
correct_response: Brilliant! Your heart is the pump that keeps you going! It beats about 70 times per minute and sends blood everywhere! Without your heart, blood wouldn't reach your brain or muscles!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is the heart. Your heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood. It's located in your chest and beats rhythmically. Every time your heart beats, it pushes blood through your body!
hint: Feel your chest. Thump thump! That's your heart beating! What organ pumps blood around your body?
mnemonic: Your heart pumps blood everywhere!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you feel your heartbeat? What helped you identify the heart?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What organ takes in air from your lungs and delivers it to your blood? It's how you get oxygen!
correct_response: Fantastic! Your lungs are like balloons that bring oxygen in! When you breathe in, air goes into your lungs and oxygen goes into your blood. Your lungs are amazing air filters!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is lungs. Your lungs are the organs that help you breathe. They take oxygen from the air and put it into your blood. You have two lungs, one on each side of your chest!
hint: Take a deep breath! Feel your chest expand. What organs are getting bigger when you breathe in?
mnemonic: Lungs bring oxygen into your blood!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember lungs? Did you think about breathing and oxygen?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call the tube that carries food from your mouth to your stomach? It's like a tunnel for food!
correct_response: Excellent! Your oesophagus is the food tube! It carries food from your mouth down to your stomach through muscle contractions - like a peristaltic wave moving food along!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is oesophagus. The oesophagus is the tube connecting your mouth to your stomach. When you swallow, food travels down this tube. It's about 25cm long in adults!
hint: Feel your throat when you swallow. Can you feel food moving down? What carries food from your mouth to your stomach?
mnemonic: Oesophagus carries food to your stomach!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember the oesophagus? Did you think about swallowing food?

subject: Science
read_aloud: Your brain has billions of tiny cells called neurons. What do we call the special junction where two neurons meet to pass signals? It's like a handshake for information!
correct_response: Incredible! A synapse is where neurons talk to each other! They're the tiny gaps where electrical or chemical signals jump from one neuron to another - billions of these connections let you think, learn, and remember!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is synapse. Neurons are brain cells. They have long connections called axons. At the end of each axon, there's a tiny gap called a synapse where signals pass to another neuron. These connections are how your brain works!
hint: Think about neurons passing messages. They're like telephone wires carrying signals. Where does the message jump from one neuron to another?
mnemonic: Synapses are where neurons connect!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember synapses? Did you think about how brain cells communicate?

subject: Science
read_aloud: How many senses do humans have? Think about sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch!
correct_response: Perfect! Humans have five senses - sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch! Your eyes, ears, nose, tongue, and skin all work together to help you understand the world!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Humans have exactly five traditional senses. Sight lets you see. Hearing lets you listen. Smell lets you detect odours. Taste helps you enjoy food. Touch lets you feel textures. That's five senses working together!
hint: Count on your fingers: one for eyes, two for ears, one for nose, one for tongue. That's five senses!
mnemonic: Five senses help you understand the world!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you name all five senses? What helped you remember there are exactly five?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call the hard outer layer of Earth that we walk on? Under our feet there's solid rock and soil!
correct_response: Brilliant! Earth's crust is the solid outer layer we walk on! It's made of solid rock and soil - the ground beneath your feet! Cities, farms, and forests all sit on the crust!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is crust. The outermost layer of Earth is the crust. It's solid rock, usually a few kilometres thick. We build our houses, roads, and farms on this layer!
hint: Think about what's under your feet when you stand on the ground. Solid rock and soil. What do we call this hard outer layer?
mnemonic: Crust is the hard outer layer we walk on!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember the crust? Did you think about what we build on the ground?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call the layer beneath the crust that is hot and partly melted? It's like Earth's thick, hot soup layer!
correct_response: Excellent! The mantle is the thick layer beneath the crust! It's made of hot rock that can flow slowly - over millions of years, mountains and continents even move on this flowing layer!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is mantle. Beneath the Earth's crust is the mantle. It's much thicker than the crust - about 2,900km thick! The mantle is hot and can flow very slowly, which is why continents can move around over millions of years!
hint: Think about volcanoes. Magma from deep inside pushes up to make volcanoes. What layer is the hot, partly melted one?
mnemonic: Mantle is the hot layer beneath the crust!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember the mantle? Did you think about volcanoes or moving continents?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call the very centre of Earth made of hot, dense metal? It's Earth's super hot, heavy heart!
correct_response: Amazing! Earth's core is the super hot centre made mostly of iron and nickel! It's about 5,400°C - as hot as the surface of the Sun! The core creates Earth's magnetic field!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is core. The very centre of Earth is the core. It's a ball of mostly iron and nickel, around 1,220km in radius. It's incredibly hot - about 5,400°C, almost as hot as the Sun's surface!
hint: Think about the deepest mine you can imagine. What's at the very centre of Earth? Super hot metal!
mnemonic: Core is Earth's super hot centre!
metacognitive_prompt: What clue helped you remember the core? Did you think about the centre being hottest?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call water that falls from clouds as rain, snow, or hail? It's precipitation from the sky!
correct_response: Brilliant! Precipitation is water falling from clouds! Rain, snow, sleet, and hail - they're all types of precipitation that water our plants and fill our reservoirs!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is precipitation. When water in clouds gets too heavy, it falls to the ground. Rain is liquid drops. Snow is ice crystals. Hail is balls of ice. We call all of these precipitation - water falling from the sky!
hint: Think about raindrops falling. What do we call water falling from clouds?
mnemonic: Precipitation is water falling from clouds!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember precipitation? Did you think about different types of falling water?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call the moving air that brings different weather? It can be a gentle breeze or a powerful storm!
correct_response: Perfect! Wind is moving air! Sometimes it's a gentle breeze rustling leaves, and sometimes it's a powerful storm pushing trees over! Wind moves from high pressure areas to low pressure areas!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is wind. Wind is moving air. You can feel it on your face. Sometimes it's a soft breeze cooling you down, and sometimes it's a strong gust nearly blowing you over. Wind moves heat and moisture around!
hint: Think about trees bending. What moves the air and makes the trees sway?
mnemonic: Wind is moving air that brings weather!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you identify wind? Did you feel the air moving?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call it when two or more substances mix together but don't chemically react? Like mixing sand and water - they stay separate!
correct_response: Excellent! A mixture is when substances are mixed together but not chemically bonded! Trail mix is a mixture - sand and water mixed together but not chemically reacted. You can separate them back out again!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is mixture. When you mix things like sand and water, they don't form a new substance through chemical reaction. The sand stays as sand grains, and the water stays as water. They're just physically mixed together!
hint: Think about trail mix. What happens when you mix sand and water? Can you separate them again?
mnemonic: Mixtures are physically mixed but not chemically reacted!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember mixtures? Did you think about separating mixtures back out?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call a change where one substance reacts with oxygen to produce energy, usually with heat and light? Hint: think about burning wood or fuel in a car engine!
correct_response: Brilliant! Combustion is when something reacts with oxygen to produce energy! Burning wood in a fireplace, fuel in a car engine, or a candle flame - all produce heat and light through combustion!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is combustion. Combustion needs three things: fuel, oxygen, and heat energy. When wood burns, it reacts with oxygen in the air. This reaction produces heat and light. Cars use combustion in their engines!
hint: Think about a campfire. What happens to the wood when it burns? It produces energy as heat and light!
mnemonic: Combustion produces energy from fuel and oxygen!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember combustion? Did you think about burning or car engines?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call it when you notice something interesting and make a guess about why it happens? Then you test your guess to see if it's right!
correct_response: Perfect! Observation is when you carefully notice something and then make predictions! Scientists start by observing - looking closely, measuring, and recording what they see. Then they make guesses about why it happens and design experiments to test them!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is observation. Observation is the first step in science. Looking carefully at things, measuring them, and recording what you notice. Scientists don't just guess - they observe first, then make educated guesses, then test those guesses!
hint: Think about a scientist in a lab coat. What do they do first when they want to understand something?
mnemonic: Scientists observe first, then make predictions!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember observation? Did you think about observing before guessing?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call a testable prediction that can be proved or disproved by doing an experiment? It's like a smart guess that you can check!
correct_response: Excellent! A hypothesis is a testable prediction! After observing something, scientists make a hypothesis - an educated guess about what might happen. Then they design an experiment to test it - to prove if the hypothesis is right or wrong!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is hypothesis. A hypothesis is a prediction that you can test. It's not just a wild guess - it's based on your observations and knowledge. Scientists then do experiments to see if their hypothesis was correct!
hint: Think about predicting what will happen. After you observe something, what do you think might happen? Then you test it!
mnemonic: Hypotheses are testable predictions!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember hypothesis? Did you think about making predictions and testing them?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call a special skill or ability that someone develops through lots of practice and training? Like learning to play piano or ride a bike!
correct_response: Wonderful! A skill is something you get better at through practice! You can learn skills like playing piano, riding a bike, or playing football through lots of training. Practice makes perfect!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is skill. Skills are abilities you develop. Reading is a skill. Maths is a skill. Football is a skill. You get better at these through practice and training. Nobody is born knowing everything - you have to learn!
hint: Think about something you're good at now. Did you always know how to do it, or did you have to learn?
mnemonic: Skills improve with practice and training!
metacognitive_prompt: What skills have you developed through practice? What helped you remember the difference between innate and learned?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What unit do we use to measure how much water a bottle can hold? Hint: think about bottles in your kitchen or drinks!
correct_response: Brilliant! Litres measure volume - how much space something takes up! A large bottle of water might hold 2 litres. We measure liquids in litres and millilitres - it tells us the volume!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is litres. We measure the volume of liquids. When you buy a drink, it might say '500ml' on the bottle. That's half a litre. Water bottles are measured in litres - big ones are 1 or 2 litres!
hint: Think about water bottles. What does 'ml' or 'L' mean on a drink bottle?
mnemonic: Litres measure the volume of liquids!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember litres? Did you think about water bottle labels?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What unit do scientists use to measure length? Hint: centimetres, metres, or kilometres! You use them to measure height, distance, or how tall something is!
correct_response: Excellent! Metres measure length or distance! We measure big distances in kilometres or metres, and small things in centimetres or millimetres! Your height might be 1.4 metres, or 140 centimetres!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is metres. We measure length and distance in metres. Long distances are kilometres. Short distances are metres. Very small things might be measured in millimetres or centimetres. Your height in metres or centimetres!
hint: Think about a ruler. What unit has 'm' after it? Metres measure length!
mnemonic: Metres measure length and distance!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember metres? Did you think about using a ruler or measuring tape?

subject: Science
read_aloud: Everything in the world is made of tiny building blocks called atoms. What do we call two or more atoms joined together? Is it A, molecule, compound, or element?
correct_response: Incredible! A molecule is two or more atoms joined together! Water is a molecule (H2O) with two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom! Carbon dioxide (CO2) is a molecule too!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is molecule. Atoms are the tiny building blocks. When atoms join together, they make molecules. Water is H2O - that's a molecule. Carbon dioxide is CO2 - that's a molecule. Two or more atoms make a molecule!
hint: Think about water. H2O means two hydrogen atoms joined with one oxygen. That's a molecule!
mnemonic: Molecules are atoms joined together!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember molecules? Did you think about water being H2O?

subject: Science
read_aloud: What do we call a substance made of only ONE type of atom? Gold is pure gold, oxygen is pure oxygen - they're elements! What's the word?
correct_response: Amazing! An element is a substance made of only one type of atom! Gold is an element - it's only gold atoms. Oxygen is an element - it's only oxygen atoms. Elements are pure substances that can't be broken down into simpler substances by normal chemical means!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The answer is element. Elements are pure substances. Gold is gold. Silver is silver. Oxygen is oxygen. Elements are made of only one type of atom - you can't separate gold into anything simpler! Water (H2O) is NOT an element - it's a compound of hydrogen and oxygen!
hint: Think about gold jewellery. Gold is only one thing - it's an element!
mnemonic: Elements are made of one type of atom!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember elements? Did you think about gold or pure substances?
